10.note: Look up at the sky and count the stars — if indeed you can count them. So shall your offspring be — Moses's declaration that Israel has become as numerous as the stars of heaven is a direct fulfillment report of the Abrahamic promise. The covenant count (stars of heaven) that Abraham received is now realized at the threshold of the land: the forty-year wilderness generation is itself the evidence of divine faithfulness to the Abrahamic oath.

17.note: My brothers and sisters, believers in our glorious Lord Jesus Christ must not show favoritism — James's prohibition of partiality in the Christian assembly echoes the judicial instruction of Deuteronomy 1:17: you shall not show partiality in judgment; hear the small and the great alike. Both texts address the same temptation (differential treatment based on status) and ground the prohibition in the same logic: the judgment ultimately belongs to God, who is no respecter of persons.

31.note: In all their distress he too was distressed, and the angel of his presence saved them. In his love and mercy he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old — the father-son carrying image of Deuteronomy 1:31 (as a man carries his son) is the paradigm that Isaiah develops into the theology of YHWH's own suffering-solidarity with Israel in the wilderness. The carrying is not merely parental tenderness but divine identification with the carried one's burden.

It was I who taught Ephraim to walk, taking them by the arms... I led them with cords of kindness, with bands of love, and I was to them like one who eases the yoke on their jaws — Hosea's retrospect on the exodus uses the father-child carrying and leading imagery that Deuteronomy 1:31 articulates. Moses's single image of YHWH carrying Israel like a son becomes in Hosea 11 a sustained portrait of parental attachment and tender instruction.

32.note: Do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion, during the time of testing in the desert, where your ancestors tested and tried me — the Kadesh-barnea failure narrated in Deuteronomy 1:32-46 (yet in spite of all this you did not trust the LORD) is the specific episode Hebrews holds up as the paradigmatic warning for the new covenant community. The failure to enter the land through unbelief becomes the type of failing to enter God's rest.

35.note: So I declared on oath in my anger: They shall never enter my rest — the oath that bars the faithless generation from the land (Deuteronomy 1:35: not one of the men of this wicked generation shall see the good land) is the same oath cited in Psalms 95:11 and then in Hebrews 3:11. The Deuteronomic verdict becomes the Psalmic warning and then the Hebrews eschatological application: the rest still remains for those who trust.

7.note: My God will meet all your needs according to the riches of his glory in Christ Jesus — Paul's promise to the Philippians is grounded in the covenant-provision principle Moses articulates in Deuteronomy 2:7: these forty years the LORD your God has been with you; you have not lacked anything. The wilderness provision of YHWH for the full duration of the wilderness period is the OT paradigm for the apostolic confidence that the God who provided in the desert continues to provide in mission.

14.note: And with whom was he angry for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose bodies perished in the wilderness? — the thirty-eight year attrition of the faithless generation (Deuteronomy 2:14: until the entire generation of warriors had perished from the camp) is cited in Hebrews as the result of God's anger at unbelief. The generation's physical dying-off becomes in Hebrews a type of the spiritual consequence of persistent rebellion against the covenant word.

25.note: Rahab said to the men: I know that the LORD has given you this land and that a great fear of you has fallen on us — the YHWH-induced terror that Deuteronomy 2:25 predicts (I will begin to put the dread and fear of you upon all peoples under heaven) is confirmed by Rahab's testimony in Joshua 2: the Canaanites have heard and their hearts melted. The divine promise of anticipatory terror is fulfilled in the Jericho testimony before any Israelite sword is drawn.

30.note: Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden — Paul's theological statement about divine sovereignty in Romans 9:18 draws on the hardening pattern that Deuteronomy 2:30 illustrates: the LORD your God made Sihon's spirit obstinate and his heart hard. The Pharaoh-hardening logic (already seen in Exodus) recurs here with Sihon; Paul generalizes the pattern into the principle of sovereign divine freedom in mercy and hardening.

24.note: Among the gods there is none like you, Lord; no deeds can compare with yours. All the nations you have made will come and worship before you... for you are great and do marvelous deeds; you alone are God — Moses's prayer in Deuteronomy 3:24 (what god in heaven or on earth can do such works as yours?) is the progenitor of the comparative-monotheism formula that Psalm 86 develops. Both texts move from the incomparability of YHWH's deeds to the logical conclusion of his sole deity.

28.note: Be strong and courageous, because you will lead these people to inherit the land I swore to their ancestors — the LORD's commissioning of Joshua in Josh 1 is the direct fulfillment of Moses's command in Deuteronomy 3:28: commission Joshua, and encourage him and strengthen him, for he shall cross over at the head of this people. The triple be strong-and-courageous of Joshua 1 fulfills the triple mandate Moses received here to commission, encourage, and strengthen his successor.

2.note: I warn everyone who hears the words of the prophecy of this scroll: if anyone adds anything to them, God will add to that person the plagues described in this scroll... if anyone takes words away from this scroll of prophecy, God will take away from that person any share in the tree of life — Revelation's closing warning against addition or subtraction from its words is the canonical application of the Deuteronomic canonical-sealing formula of Deuteronomy 4:2 (do not add to the word I command you, nor take from it) to the final book of the NT canon.

12.note: And the Father who sent me has himself testified concerning me. You have never heard his voice or seen his form — Jesus's statement that no one has heard YHWH's voice or seen his form directly invokes the Sinai theophany of Deuteronomy 4:12 (you heard the sound of words but saw no form; there was only a voice) as the paradigmatic divine encounter. Jesus implicitly positions himself as the unprecedented visible form of YHWH: in contrast to Sinai, in him the invisible God has become seeable and hearable in a new mode.

24.note: For our God is a consuming fire — Hebrews quotes Deuteronomy 4:24 directly in the context of warning the new covenant community not to treat the gospel's grace as license for indifference. The consuming-fire nature of YHWH is not mitigated in the new covenant; rather, the greater privilege of approaching the heavenly Zion (vs. earthly Sinai) increases rather than decreases the gravity of rejecting the speaking God.

29.note: You will seek me and find me when you seek me with all your heart — Jeremiah's letter to the Babylonian exiles quotes Deuteronomy 4:29 nearly verbatim (from there you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search for him with all your heart). The Mosaic promise of post-scattering restoration through wholehearted seeking becomes Jeremiah's direct pastoral promise to the exiles: the worst-case Deuteronomic scenario is not the end of the covenant.

35.note: I am the LORD, and there is no other; apart from me there is no God... so that from the rising of the sun to the place of its setting people may know there is none besides me — Second Isaiah's sustained monotheistic proclamation (there is no other) is the prophetic extension of the Deuteronomic formula first stated in Deuteronomy 4:35: the LORD is God; there is no other besides him. The Deuteronomic exclusive monotheism becomes the theological spine of Isaiah 40-55.

We know that there is no God but one — Paul's practical premise for the idol-food discussion in 1 Corinthians 8 reproduces the Deuteronomic monotheism of Deuteronomy 4:35. The heis theos (one God) confession Paul invokes is the Greek articulation of what Moses established at Sinai: the LORD alone is God; no other being shares his divine status, so idols receive no genuine honor when Israel sacrifices.

37.note: You did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit — Jesus's reminder of divine initiative in election expresses the same theological logic as Deuteronomy 4:37: because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants. In both texts, the initiative belongs entirely to YHWH/Christ; the chosen party does not earn or initiate the relationship but finds itself already loved and appointed.

6.note: I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery — the Decalogue's historical prologue frames divine authority in terms of redemptive act, not raw power. Zechariah's Benedictus uses the same framework: God has rescued us from the hand of our enemies, to serve him without fear, as he promised.

7.note: You shall have no other gods before me — Jesus's response to the devil's offer of all the kingdoms of the world ('worship me') draws on both Deuteronomy 6:13 ('fear the LORD your God and serve him only') and implicitly on the first commandment; exclusive worship of God is the covenant foundation Jesus defends in the wilderness.

The angel's rebuke of John when he falls to worship ('Do not do that! Worship God!') enforces the first commandment's exclusivity. Revelation's insistence on God-alone worship throughout reflects the Deuteronomic foundation laid here.

9.note: The visiting of iniquity on subsequent generations is the same formulation as Exodus 20:5 — the Deuteronomic Decalogue reproduces it verbatim; the NT understanding of this principle is nuanced: Ezek 18 insists on individual accountability, while Jesus's death breaks the inherited-consequence pattern through forgiveness.

16.note: Paul explicitly cites 'Honor your father and mother' as 'the first commandment with a promise' (Ephesians 6:2), quoting the Deuteronomic form 'that it may go well with you and that you may live long in the land.' The Deuteronomic covenant-land promise attached to the fifth commandment becomes in Paul the promise attached to childhood obedience within the new covenant household.

Jesus cites the fifth commandment (honoring parents) against the Pharisees who allow corban-dedication to override the duty to parents — the commandment's binding force on covenant-community relationships is precisely what Jesus defends.

17.note: You shall not murder — Jesus cites this commandment in the Sermon on the Mount ('You have heard it said to those of old...') and radicalizes it to the level of anger and contempt. The Deuteronomic sixth commandment is the launching point for the first antithesis.

Paul summarizes the social commandments of the Decalogue — including 'You shall not murder' — under the principle 'Love your neighbor as yourself.' Deuteronomy's commandments are the specific content that neighbor-love fulfills.

18.note: You shall not commit adultery — Jesus cites this commandment in the second antithesis of the Sermon on the Mount, radicalizing the seventh commandment from the act of adultery to the inner disposition of lustful looking. The Deuteronomic prohibition is the text Jesus interprets and intensifies.

James cites both 'Do not commit adultery' and 'Do not murder' as examples of commandments from 'him who said' — the single divine lawgiver. Breaking one commandment makes one liable under the whole law; the Decalogue is treated as a unified whole.

21.note: Paul identifies the commandment 'You shall not covet' as the specific Decalogue word that revealed sin in him: 'I would not have known what covetousness was if the law had not said you shall not covet.' The Deuteronomic form of the tenth commandment (which specifically adds 'wife,' 'field,' and 'male servant' to the Exodus list) is the form Paul would have encountered in synagogue catechesis.

Paul includes 'You shall not covet' in his summary of Decalogue commandments that are fulfilled in love of neighbor — the covetousness prohibition is the inward commandment that disciplines desire toward others.

22.note: These words the LORD spoke out of the fire, the cloud, and the thick darkness, with a loud voice — Hebrews 12 explicitly contrasts the Sinai theophany (fire, darkness, trumpet blast, voice) with the approach to Mount Zion through Christ. The Deuteronomic description of the Sinai-voice experience is the OT counterpart to the new covenant gathering Hebrews describes.

29.note: If only they had such a heart as this always, to fear me and keep all my commandments — this divine lament expresses exactly what Moses's heart-circumcision passages (Deuteronomy 10:16; Deuteronomy 30:6) and Jeremiah's new covenant promise address: the inner renewal that makes the law-keeping God longs for possible. The condition God wishes Israel had becomes, in the new covenant, the condition God creates.

4.note: Jesus cites the Shema as the first and greatest commandment — 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one' — in response to a scribe's question (Mark 12:29-30). The Shema provides the theological foundation for exclusive love of God; Jesus presents it not as one commandment among many but as the first command from which all others derive.

Paul restructures the Shema Christologically: 'yet for us there is one God the Father... and one Lord Jesus Christ.' He does not cite Deuteronomy 6:4 explicitly but maps the one-God-one-Lord structure of the Shema onto a two-person formula that places Jesus within the divine identity. This is the most profound early Christological use of the Shema.

You believe that God is one; you do well — James appeals to monotheism (the Shema's content) as something even demons affirm, in order to press past bare intellectual assent to the obedient faith that the Shema demands. The Shema's claim is deployed as the baseline from which genuine faith must exceed.

Hear O Israel the LORD our God the LORD is one — Jesus cites the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4-5) as the first and greatest commandment; the Shema frames the entire law in the context of YHWH's singular Lordship over Israel

5.note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:5 verbatim as the greatest commandment: 'You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.' Matthew's addition of 'mind' (dianoia) to the Markan 'heart, soul, and strength' may reflect the LXX's alternate rendering. Jesus presents the Shema's love-command as the summary of the whole law.

The lawyer who correctly summarizes the law quotes Deuteronomy 6:5 together with Leviticus 19:18 — the same pairing Jesus makes in Matthew 22:37-39. Jesus affirms the summary and connects it to the parable of the Good Samaritan, applying Deuteronomy's love-of-God to the question of who qualifies as neighbor.

13.note: You shall fear the LORD your God. You shall serve him — Jesus quotes the second half of Deuteronomy 6:13 in response to the devil's temptation to worship him: 'It is written: worship the Lord your God and serve him only.' Where Israel in the wilderness failed the test of exclusive loyalty to YHWH (golden calf, Baalism), Jesus as the true Israel restores covenant faithfulness by refusing false worship.
Worship the Lord your God only — Jesus quotes in temptation

16.note: You shall not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah — Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:16 in response to the devil's temptation to jump from the Temple pinnacle and force divine rescue: 'It is also written: do not put the Lord your God to the test.' Israel demanded miraculous proof at Massah (Exodus 17:7); Jesus refuses to demand proof from God, trusting the Father without coercion.
Do not put the Lord to test — Jesus quotes in temptation

20.note: When your son asks what is the meaning of the testimonies and statutes — the catechetical pattern of parental explanation of God's saving acts anticipates the NT call to give a defense (apologia) to everyone who asks about the hope within. Both texts envision a questioning outsider and a community prepared to explain the meaning of its identity and practices.

6.note: You are a people holy to the LORD your God; the LORD has chosen you to be his treasured possession (segullah) — Peter applies all three designations directly to the church: 'you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession' (combining Exodus 19:5-6 with Deuteronomy 7:6). Israel's election-vocabulary is transferred to the new covenant community without remainder; the church is the people of God whose identity is defined by the Deuteronomic election.

Christ gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his own possession (periousion) — Paul's word periousion is the LXX translation of segullah (treasured possession) in Deuteronomy 7:6. The Deuteronomic election of Israel as God's special possession is reapplied to the church purchased by Christ's blood.

7.note: The LORD did not set his love on you because you were more numerous — you were the smallest of all peoples — the paradox of God choosing the numerically insignificant is the OT ground for Paul's theology of divine election by grace: God chose the foolish, weak, and lowly things of the world to shame the strong, so that no one may boast before him. The Deuteronomic logic of election-against-greatness is generalized by Paul to the cross-shaped community.

8.note: Because the LORD loved you and was keeping the oath he swore to your fathers — Ephesians 1:4-5 grounds election in identical terms: chosen in love before the foundation of the world, predestined according to the pleasure of his will. The Deuteronomic grounding of Israel's election in divine love and prior oath (not human merit) is the OT pattern that Paul's theology of grace-election continues and universalizes.

The principle 'God's purpose in election might stand, not based on works but on him who calls' — Paul's discussion of the divine election of Jacob over Esau applies the same Deuteronomic logic: God chose the lesser before any works were done, in order to demonstrate that election is of grace, not human advantage.

9.note: Know that the LORD your God is God — the faithful God who keeps his covenant and steadfast love — Paul's foundational assertion 'God is faithful' (1 Corinthians 1:9; Deuteronomy 10:13; 2 Corinthians 1:18) draws on this Deuteronomic characterization of God as the covenant-keeping faithful one. Divine faithfulness is not a NT innovation but the Deuteronomic core of Israel's covenant theology.

If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive — the divine faithfulness that grounds the promise of forgiveness in 1 John is the same quality that Deuteronomy 7:9 names as definitional for YHWH: he is the faithful God who keeps his covenant. The character that Deuteronomy declares is the basis on which NT forgiveness-promises are grounded.

25.note: Do not covet the silver or gold on the carved images — nothing abominable or detestable (toevah) was to enter Israel's covenant space; Revelation 21:27 declares that nothing abominable (bdelykton — the LXX translation of toevah) will enter the new Jerusalem. Deuteronomy's covenant-purity requirement becomes the criterion for the eschatological city.

3.note: Jesus answered, It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God — Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 verbatim in response to the first wilderness temptation. The manna-testing episode (YHWH humbled Israel by letting them hunger, then fed them to teach dependence on the divine word) becomes the script Jesus enacts perfectly: where Israel failed the bread test, Jesus trusts YHWH's provision and word.

I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry — the manna theology of Deuteronomy 8:3 (the bread of the divine word) reaches its Christological climax in John 6. Jesus is not merely the teacher of the divine word but is himself the living bread that came down from heaven, surpassing and fulfilling the manna that Moses mediated.
Man shall not live by bread alone — Jesus quotes in temptation

5.note: Endure hardship as discipline; God is treating you as his children. For what children are not disciplined by their father? — Hebrews develops the father-son discipline principle that Deuteronomy 8:5 establishes as the interpretive framework for covenant suffering. Moses's explanation that YHWH's testing-in-the-wilderness is paternal discipline (as a man disciplines his son) becomes the NT rationale for the suffering of the people of God: it is not punishment but formation.

17.note: What do you have that you did not receive? And if you did receive it, why do you boast as though you did not? — Paul's anti-boasting challenge in 1 Corinthians 4 is the NT application of the Deuteronomic warning: beware of saying in your heart, My power and the strength of my hands produced this wealth for me. Both texts diagnose the same spiritual error: attributing to the creature what belongs to the Creator.

4.note: For by grace you have been saved, through faith — and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God — not by works, so that no one can boast — Paul's foundational statement of salvation by grace and not works is the NT theological articulation of the principle Moses establishes in Deuteronomy 9:4-6: do not say in your heart that my righteousness brought me into the land. The inheritance is given by grace (confirmed covenant) not earned by merit; the NT applies the same principle to the new covenant.

9.note: After fasting forty days and forty nights, he was hungry. The tempter came to him — Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness recapitulates the Mosaic forty-day fast in Deuteronomy 9:9 (I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights, eating no bread and drinking no water). As Moses received the Torah-tablets after his fast, Jesus demonstrates his covenant faithfulness under testing. Where Moses's intercession followed Israel's failure, Jesus succeeds where Israel failed.

26.note: Did God reject his people? By no means! I am an Israelite myself, a descendant of Abraham, from the tribe of Benjamin. God did not reject his people, whom he foreknew — Paul's rhetorical denial of Israel's permanent rejection draws on the same logic Moses uses in Deuteronomy 9:26-29: O Lord GOD, do not destroy your people and your inheritance. Moses appeals to the Abrahamic covenant and YHWH's reputation; Paul appeals to divine foreknowledge and election. Both resist the conclusion that YHWH has abandoned Israel.

12.note: He has shown you, O mortal, what is good. And what does the LORD require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God — Micah's prophetic summary of the covenant requirement echoes the rhetorical structure of Deuteronomy 10:12 (what does the LORD your God ask of you except to fear him, walk in his ways, love him, serve him with all your heart and soul). Both texts answer the question what does YHWH really want? by cutting through ritual specifics to describe a whole-life covenant orientation.

16.note: Circumcise yourselves to the LORD, circumcise your hearts and do not let your hearts become uncircumcised — Jeremiah's call for circumcision of the heart is the direct prophetic extension of Moses's command in Deuteronomy 10:16. What Deuteronomy commands as a spiritual-ethical imperative (circumcise your hearts and stop being stubborn), Jeremiah reissues as an eschatological urgency before judgment; the same command runs through the prophets until it becomes a divine promise (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26).

Circumcision is circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code — Paul's theological conclusion on circumcision in Romans 2 is the NT fulfillment of the Deuteronomic command of Deuteronomy 10:16. What Moses commanded as a human imperative and Jeremiah announced as an eschatological requirement, Paul declares as accomplished by the Spirit: the circumcised heart that Deuteronomy demanded is given by God in the new covenant.

17.note: They will wage war against the Lamb, but the Lamb will triumph over them because he is Lord of lords and King of kings — the divine title Lord of lords that Deuteronomy 10:17 applies to YHWH (the LORD your God is God of gods and Lord of lords) is transferred in Revelation to Jesus the Lamb. The exclusive sovereignty that belongs to YHWH in Deuteronomy 10:17 is the same sovereignty Christ exercises in the eschatological battle; the Lamb is identified with the LORD by sharing his unique title.

18.note: Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress — James's definition of pure religion directly applies the Deuteronomic ethic of Deuteronomy 10:18 (YHWH upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner). The social priorities that flow from YHWH's own character (he defends the fatherless, widow, and stranger) become the defining marks of authentic covenant community for James.

15.note: A prophet like me will the LORD your God raise up for you — Peter cites Deuteronomy 18:15 as fulfilled in Jesus; the eschatological prophet-like-Moses was the figure Israel expected, and Peter declares Jesus to be that prophet

God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers — Stephen's speech identifies the prophet-like-Moses promise as the Christological center of Moses's ministry; Israel's rejection of Moses typifies their rejection of Jesus
The Prophet who is to come into the world

18.note: I will put my words in his mouth and he shall speak to them all that I command him — Jesus explicitly fulfills this: 'I have not spoken on my own authority, but the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak'; the prophet-like-Moses receives and transmits the divine word without independent addition.

My teaching is not mine but his who sent me — the criterion of the true prophet (speaking only what YHWH commands) is what Jesus claims for his own teaching; his words are the Father's words, meeting the Deuteronomic test of the genuine prophet.

19.note: And it shall be that every soul who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed from the people — Peter cites this judgment-clause immediately after quoting Deuteronomy 18:15-16 (Acts 3:22-23), making the stakes of rejecting Jesus explicit: this is not merely a social failure but the covenant cut-off that Moses warned of.

15.note: Every matter established by two or three witnesses

4.note: The city of refuge for the manslayer who killed without intent — the institution shadows Christ as refuge: the one who flees to the city is safe from the avenger of blood as long as he stays. Hebrews uses the same 'fled for refuge' language for believers clinging to the hope set before them (Hebrews 6:18), drawing on this Mosaic institution as the conceptual background for salvation-security in Christ.

16.note: If a malicious witness rises against a man to accuse him of wrongdoing — the trial of Jesus directly violates this law: the chief priests sought false testimony against him to put him to death, and false witnesses came forward with contradictory charges (Matthew 26:59-61). The law designed to protect the innocent from false accusation is weaponized against the innocent one; the very statute whose violation it describes is what condemns him.

23.note: Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree — Paul cites Deuteronomy 21:23 as fulfilled in the crucifixion: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree; the cross is the site of curse-absorption
Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree

4.note: The elders shall wash their hands over the heifer and declare: our hands did not shed this blood — Pilate's handwashing ('I am innocent of this man's blood') directly echoes this Deuteronomic ritual for declaring corporate innocence over unavenged innocent blood. The irony is total: Pilate performs the absolution-rite over the one whose death is the very atonement the heifer-ritual could only shadow.

8.note: Accept atonement for your people Israel and do not place the guilt of innocent blood in the midst of your people — the heifer ritual addresses blood-guilt that cannot be traced to a human killer, requiring a corporate act of atonement. Hebrews draws the contrast: if the blood of bulls can cleanse outward defilement, how much more will the blood of Christ purify the conscience from dead works. The heifer-atonement is the shadow; Christ's blood is the substance.

1.note: He who divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce — Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 24:1 in the Sermon on the Mount and intensifies it: whoever divorces his wife except for sexual immorality makes her commit adultery; the Mosaic concession to hardness of heart (Matthew 19:8) is now superseded by the original creation design (Genesis 2:24)

Why then did Moses command to give a certificate of divorce? — the Pharisees cite Deuteronomy 24:1 to challenge Jesus; he responds that Moses permitted it because of hardness of heart but from the beginning it was not so; the Deuteronomic legislation is placed in the category of accommodation, not ideal

14.note: You shall not oppress a hired servant who is poor and needy, whether he is one of your brothers or one of the sojourners who are in your land — James applies the same principle: behold the wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out against you; the Lord of hosts has heard their cry; Deuteronomic labor justice is eschatological covenant obligation

16.note: Fathers shall not be put to death because of their children, nor shall children be put to death because of their fathers. Each one shall be put to death for his own sin — when the disciples ask 'who sinned, this man or his parents?', Jesus refuses the hereditary-guilt framework; Deuteronomy 24:16 and Ezekiel 18:20 establish individual moral accountability, which Christ applies to correct a distorted theodicy

4.note: Do not muzzle an ox while it treads out grain
Do not muzzle an ox -- the worker deserves his wages

5.note: If brothers dwell together and one of them dies and has no son, his brother shall take the widow as wife and perform the duty of a husband's brother to her — the Sadducees cite levirate marriage to Jesus to argue against resurrection: whose wife will she be after seven brothers each married and died? Jesus's answer (in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage) reveals that the levirate institution, designed to preserve the family name in the old covenant, is superseded in the resurrection order

26.note: Cursed is everyone who does not continue in the law

1.note: Keep the whole commandment — Jesus: I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it; not one jot will pass from the law until all is accomplished

2.note: Set up large stones and plaster them and write on them all the words of this law — Paul: you are a letter written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on stone tablets but on tablets of human hearts

3.note: Write on them all the words of this law when you cross over — the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ

6.note: Build an altar of whole stones and offer burnt offerings — Paul: present your bodies as living sacrifices, holy and pleasing to God — your true and proper worship

8.note: Write on the stones all the words of this law very plainly — whoever belongs to God hears what God says; the written law now heard through the living word, Jesus

15.note: Cursed is the man who carves an image — we should not think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image made by human design and skill

19.note: Cursed is the one who perverts justice for the sojourner, fatherless, and widow — pure religion is this: to care for orphans and widows in their distress

12.note: Do not say in your heart who will go up to heaven — Paul adapts Deuteronomy 30:12-14 Christologically: the word that is near you, in your heart and mouth, is the word of faith we proclaim; what Deuteronomy said of the Torah-command is now said of Christ and his gospel
The word is near you — righteousness by faith

6.note: Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you

21.note: I will make you jealous of those who are not a nation — Paul cites the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:21) as the OT basis for the Gentile mission provoking Israel to jealousy; the unexpected reversal of Gentile blessing is Moses's own warning
I will make you envious by a nation not a nation

35.note: It is mine to avenge; I will repay

43.note: Rejoice O Gentiles with his people — Paul cites Deuteronomy 32:43 LXX as one of four OT texts (Romans 15:9-12) proving that Gentile inclusion in the worship of God was always the divine plan from Moses through the Psalms and Isaiah
Rejoice, you Gentiles, with his people

1.note: Deuteronomy's foundational equation of covenant love with commandment-keeping anticipates Jesus's identical formula: loving him means keeping his commandments.

26.note: The blessing-and-curse binary set before Israel at Gerizim and Ebal is the typological framework Paul uses to explain Christ becoming accursed so that believers are transferred to the blessing side of the covenant.

5.note: The place YHWH chooses to make his name dwell is the type of Christ as the true temple; Jesus identifies his own body as the singular dwelling of the divine Name where true sacrifice is offered.

11.note: The centralized-worship-at-one-chosen-place principle reaches its fulfillment and transcendence when Jesus declares that true worship is now in spirit and truth, no longer tied to any geographic sanctuary.

1.note: The Mosaic false-prophet test — signs and wonders used to lead Israel after other gods — is the OT template for Jesus's end-time warning that false christs will perform great signs in order to lead even the elect astray.

5.note: Paul quotes the Deuteronomic purge-the-evil-from-among-you formula directly to instruct the Corinthian church on excommunicating the impenitent, grounding new-covenant church discipline in Mosaic covenant law.

1.note: You are children of YHWH your God — John develops sonship of God as the NT community's defining identity

2.note: A holy people to YHWH, his treasured possession — Peter applies this directly to the church as God's special people

3.note: Do not eat any detestable thing — Jesus declares all foods clean, ending the food-law boundary between Jew and Gentile
Peter's vision: what God has made clean, do not call unclean — direct reversal of the Mosaic clean/unclean distinction

The clean/unclean food legislation of Deuteronomy 14:3-21 is the backdrop for Peter's vision: all animals let down in the sheet, the voice saying 'Do not call unclean what God has made clean' — the Mosaic food distinctions are declared fulfilled and transcended in Christ

Thus he declared all foods clean — Mark's parenthetical comment on Jesus's teaching about defilement (paralleling Matthew 15:17-20) is the Christological cancellation of the Deut 14 food code; the Mosaic boundary is dissolved in Christ

21.note: Distinction between resident alien and foreigner — Paul dissolves ethnic boundaries: neither Jew nor Gentile in Christ

22.note: You shall tithe all the yield of your seed — Jesus acknowledges tithing while rebuking neglect of justice, mercy, and faithfulness

29.note: The Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless and widow shall come and eat — Jesus extends this: invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind

1.note: At the end of seven years you shall grant a release — the sabbatical-year debt-release is the type of the Lord's Prayer: forgive us our debts as we forgive our debtors
Year of release echoes jubilee themes that Jesus cites from Isaiah 61:1-2 as his mission at Nazareth

4.note: There will be no poor among you — the Jerusalem church realized this: there was not a needy person among them

7.note: If there is a poor man among your brothers, do not harden your heart — John: how can the love of God be in anyone who does not help a brother in need?

11.note: For there will never cease to be poor in the land — Jesus quotes this directly: you always have the poor with you, but you will not always have me

The poor you always have with you — Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 15:11 directly in response to the disciples' complaint about the expensive ointment; the irony is that Moses's own call to open-handedness to the poor is the basis for releasing the woman from that obligation in that moment

The poor you always have with you, but you do not always have me — Jesus's direct citation of Deuteronomy 15:11 at Bethany; his physical presence is the once-for-all occasion that the debt-release legislation cannot qualify

12.note: If your Hebrew brother is sold to you... in the seventh year you shall let him go free — Paul: for freedom Christ has set us free; do not submit again to a yoke of slavery
He must go free — if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed: the ultimate jubilee-release

17.note: The slave who chooses to remain with his master has his ear pierced — willing servant of a loving master; Christ as the willing Servant who chose the cross (Isaiah 53:7)

21.note: You shall not sacrifice a blemished animal — the Passover lamb must be without defect; Christ as the lamb without blemish or defect

1.note: Observe the month of Abib and keep the Passover to YHWH — Paul: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed; therefore let us keep the feast
It was the day of Preparation of the Passover; Jesus is crucified on the Passover — the timing is the direct fulfillment of the Passover appointment

Christ our Passover has been sacrificed — Paul's citation of the Passover lamb as the type of Christ's death; Deuteronomy 16:1-8 prescribes the centralized annual Passover that Jesus fulfills as the once-for-all Passover Lamb (cf. John 1:29)

2.note: You shall sacrifice the Passover at the place YHWH will choose — Jesus suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through his own blood

3.note: You shall eat unleavened bread with it — the bread of affliction — Paul: let us keep the feast not with old leaven but with unleavened bread of sincerity and truth

8.note: On the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly — the early church gathered on the first day for the breaking of bread: the resurrection-day replaces the seventh-day solemn assembly

9.note: Count seven weeks from the day the sickle is first put to the standing grain — the Feast of Weeks (Pentecost) fulfilled at Acts 2 when the Spirit was poured out on the gathered community

When the day of Pentecost came — the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) prescribed in Deuteronomy 16:9-12 is the occasion when the Spirit is poured out on the gathered disciples; the agricultural harvest festival becomes the occasion of the Spirit-harvest of first converts

11.note: You shall rejoice before YHWH with your son and daughter, servant and maidservant, Levite, sojourner, fatherless, and widow — the universal inclusion fulfilled in Christ where all are one

13.note: You shall keep the Feast of Booths — Jesus teaches at the Feast of Booths; on the last day of the feast he cries out the living-water invitation (John 7:37-38)

16.note: Three times a year all males shall appear before YHWH at Passover, Weeks, and Booths — Jesus fulfills all three: his death at Passover, Spirit poured out at Pentecost/Weeks, living-water cry at Booths

18.note: You shall appoint judges in all your towns — the Father has given all judgment to the Son; Jesus as the eschatological judge fulfills the role of the covenant judge

20.note: Justice, and only justice, you shall follow — blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied

6.note: A person shall be put to death only on the evidence of two or three witnesses — Jesus cites the two-witness rule: in your Law it is written that the testimony of two witnesses is true; he and the Father are the two witnesses

8.note: You shall come to the priests the Levites and to the judge who is in office — Jesus honors the governing authorities while locating final authority in YHWH

14.note: When you come to the land YHWH gives you and you say let us set a king over us — Israel's request for a king is the type of the true King; Jesus is King of kings and Lord of lords

The king legislation of Deuteronomy 17:14-20 (not multiplying horses, wives, or gold; writing Torah; not lifting his heart above his brothers) is the portrait of the ideal king that Israel's history consistently failed to produce; Jesus as the true King fulfills every condition: no military apparatus (John 18:36 'my kingdom is not of this world'), no earthly wealth, perfect knowledge of Torah, servant-hearted toward his brothers

15.note: You shall set as king over you him whom YHWH your God will choose, one from among your brothers — the chosen King comes to his own and his own people did not receive him

16.note: He shall not acquire many horses or make the people return to Egypt — the law of the king prohibits accumulation of military power; Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey, not a war horse (Zechariah 9:9)

17.note: He shall not acquire many wives — the faithful King has one bride: the church, adorned as a bride for her husband at the marriage supper of the Lamb

18.note: He shall write for himself a copy of this law — the Word became flesh: the eternal Logos is the living Torah that the true King embodies perfectly
The king shall read it all the days of his life — the Son through whom God spoke in these last days is the living word that surpasses the written copy

19.note: He shall read in it all the days of his life that he may learn to fear YHWH his God — Jesus: I have kept my Father's commands and remain in his love; the true King perfectly obeys the Torah

20.note: That his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers — the king who does not exalt himself: Christ humbled himself and became obedient to death; therefore God exalted him

5.note: I have bought a field, and I must go out and see it — Luke 14:18-20's three excuses in the Great Banquet parable (field, oxen, wife) deliberately echo Deuteronomy 20:5-7's three war exemptions (built a house, planted a vineyard, betrothed a woman); Jesus reframes the banquet-refusers as those who invoke the very excuses Torah authorized for battle, suggesting they treat the kingdom invitation as less urgent than a property transaction

10.note: When you draw near to a city to fight against it, first offer it terms of peace — the king who sends a delegation to ask for terms of peace in Luke 14:32 echoes the Deuteronomic war-law; Jesus transposes the military logic into the logic of discipleship: count the cost before you begin, and know whether you can complete the campaign

Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace — Jesus weeping over Jerusalem echoes the Deuteronomic offer of peace before siege; Jerusalem's rejection of Christ is the rejection of the peace-terms YHWH's envoy came to offer before the judgment fell in 70 CE

When you draw near to a city to fight against it, first offer it terms of peace — the law requiring a peace-offer before siege-warfare lies behind Jesus's lament over Jerusalem: 'Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!' The divine Warrior has come offering peace; Jerusalem's rejection makes the coming judgment a siege she chose rather than one imposed without warning.

3.note: Do not be faint-hearted or afraid; do not be in dread or terror of them — the priest's pre-battle address grounds Israel's fearlessness in YHWH's presence. The NT takes up this covenant-presence logic: Hebrews 13:5-6 quotes Deuteronomy 31:6 and draws the same conclusion — 'the Lord is my helper; I will not fear.' The Deuteronomic assurance of the divine warrior going before Israel is reapplied to the Christian's daily battle.

4.note: For the LORD your God is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies — the logic of the divine warrior who fights on Israel's behalf is the OT background for Paul's rhetorical climax: 'If God is for us, who can be against us?' The covenant pledge to fight for Israel becomes the Spirit-age guarantee that no spiritual power can separate believers from the love of Christ.

12.note: Tassels (tzitzit) on the four corners of your garment — Jesus accuses the scribes and Pharisees of making their tassels long to be seen; the outward sign meant to remind Israel of the commandments (Numbers 15:38-39) had become a performance of piety rather than an act of covenant remembrance

The woman who had bled for twelve years touched the fringe (tzitzit) of Jesus's garment; the healing power that flowed from the hem of the Messiah's garment redeems the tassel-commandment by embodying the holiness it was meant to evoke — to touch his garment is to receive the wholeness Israel's covenant was designed to give

1.note: No one whose testicles are crushed or whose male organ is cut off shall enter the assembly of the LORD — the Ethiopian eunuch of Acts 8 was a person excluded under Deuteronomy 23:1; Philip's baptism of him is the enacted fulfillment of Isaiah 56:3-5 (I will give them a name better than sons and daughters) and signals the new covenant's removal of assembly-barriers through Christ

No distinction of male and female in Christ Jesus — the Deuteronomic assembly-exclusions that separated Israelites from the covenant community are abolished in Christ; the one new humanity (Ephesians 2:15) has no boundary-markers of exclusion

5.note: The LORD your God turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the LORD your God loved you — the Balaam curse-turned-blessing (Num 22-24) becomes a pattern that Paul applies universally: God works all things together for good for those who love him; the divine reversal of curse into blessing reaches its apex in the cross, where the supreme curse becomes the supreme blessing (Galatians 3:13-14)

25.note: You may pluck heads of grain with your hand, but you shall not put a sickle to your neighbor's standing grain — when Pharisees accuse the disciples of Sabbath-breaking for plucking grain, Jesus defends them with David's eating of the showbread; both cases apply the same Deuteronomic principle that human need has priority over external ritual restriction, and Jesus declares himself Lord even of the Sabbath

1.note: When you come to the land and bring the first of all the fruit of the ground — Christ is the firstfruits of those raised from the dead, the antitype of the first-fruit offering

2.note: First of all the fruit — God chose to give us birth through the word of truth, that we might be a kind of firstfruits of all he created

5.note: A wandering Aramean was my father — Abraham the wanderer is the father of all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike; the confession of origins points to faith, not ethnicity

8.note: YHWH brought us out of Egypt with a mighty hand — the Exodus is the type of Christian salvation and baptism; our ancestors were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea

10.note: You shall set it down before YHWH and worship — the magi set down their gifts before the child and worshiped; the first-fruit offering fulfilled in the first-born Son

12.note: You shall give it to the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow — Jesus extends the same table: invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind

16.note: YHWH commands you to do these statutes and rules; you shall do them with all your heart and soul — Jesus summarizes: love the Lord with all your heart, soul, mind; love your neighbor — all the Law hangs on these

17.note: You have declared YHWH to be your God — God gave himself to purify for himself a people who are his own possession, eager to do good works; the mutual-declaration covenant now sealed in Christ

18.note: YHWH has declared you to be his treasured possession — Peter applies this to the church: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession

19.note: He will set you high above all nations he has made, for praise and fame and honor — the nations bring their glory and honor into the new Jerusalem; Israel's exaltation is the type of the new covenant community's glory

1.note: The blessings-for-obedience covenant structure of Deuteronomy 28 sets the framework that Christ resolves: in him the blessing of Abraham reaches the Gentiles, and the Spirit is the covenant blessing received through faith rather than law-keeping.

15.note: All who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written 'cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law' — Paul draws on the curse-for-disobedience principle of Deut 28 to demonstrate that law-keeping can only condemn, not justify.

4.note: Paul directly quotes Deuteronomy 29:4 ('God gave them a spirit of stupor, eyes that would not see and ears that would not hear') to explain the partial hardening of Israel — what Moses described as Israel's wilderness inability becomes the prophesied pattern of Israel's response to the Messiah.

29.note: The secret things that belong to YHWH become the mystery now revealed through the Spirit: Paul argues that what eyes have not seen nor ears heard God has revealed through the Spirit — the Deuteronomic hiddenness of divine counsel is overcome in Christ.

2.note: YHWH came from Sinai with ten thousands of holy ones at his right hand — the angelic host at Sinai is the OT background for Paul's statement that the Torah was 'ordained through angels by an intermediary' (Galatians 3:19); Deuteronomy 33:2 LXX reads 'on his right hand were angels with him'

The flaming fire at YHWH's right hand at Sinai — Hebrews 2:2 refers to 'the message declared by angels' as the legal basis for the Mosaic law; the angelic mediation of the covenant at Sinai (Deuteronomy 33:2) establishes why the covenant received through the Son is superior to the one delivered through angels

27.note: Underneath are the everlasting arms — the eternal God as refuge with everlasting arms is the OT image for what Jesus claims in John 10:28-29: no one will snatch them out of my hand, and no one is able to snatch them out of my Father's hand; the everlasting arms of Deuteronomy 33:27 are the hands of Father and Son

5.note: Moses the servant of YHWH died there — Hebrews 3:5-6 uses Moses's faithful servanthood in the house and his death as the foil for Christ: Moses was faithful as a servant, but Christ is faithful as a Son over God's house; the finality of Moses's death (he did not enter the land) is the finality the Levitical system shared, overcome only by the Son who lives forever

YHWH buried him and no one knows the place of his burial — Michael the archangel disputed with the devil about the body of Moses (Jude 9), suggesting Moses's burial place was contested in Jewish tradition; Jude uses it as an example of not reviling spiritual powers, trusting YHWH's own rebuke

10.note: There has not arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses whom YHWH knew face to face — Deuteronomy's closing statement that no prophet like Moses has yet arisen is the setup for Peter's Pentecost sermon: Moses himself said 'God will raise up a prophet like me' (Deuteronomy 18:15), and Peter declares that Jesus is that prophet; 34:10 makes 18:15 the book's open expectation

No prophet since like Moses — Hebrews 3:3 completes the comparison: Jesus has been counted worthy of more glory than Moses, as the builder of a house has more honor than the house itself; the Torah's own closing acknowledgment of Moses's unparalleled greatness becomes the foil for the letter's central argument that Christ is categorically greater
